title: AWS infrastructure — VPC, security groups, key pair, IAM, EC2
description: Set up foundational AWS infrastructure for EC2 deployment. This is the core AWS wiring — every command maps to a concept that comes up in interviews. Create a security group with SSH/HTTP/HTTPS access, generate a key pair for SSH, create an IAM role with CloudWatch permissions, and launch a t2.micro free-tier instance with an Elastic IP.

plan.text: Verify AWS CLI is configured: run `aws sts get-caller-identity`. Confirm region is us-east-1.
acceptance_criteria: CLI returns account ID without auth errors

plan.text: Create security group named 'unpack-sg': allow inbound SSH (22), HTTP (80), HTTPS (443) from 0.0.0.0/0. Outbound all traffic.
acceptance_criteria: Security group exists with correct inbound rules
`aws ec2 describe-security-groups --group-names unpack-sg` shows 3 inbound rules

plan.text: Create EC2 key pair named 'unpack-key', save .pem to ~/.ssh/unpack-key.pem, chmod 400.
Create IAM role 'unpack-ec2-role' with AmazonSSMManagedInstanceCore and CloudWatchLogsFullAccess policies. Create instance profile and attach role.
acceptance_criteria: IAM role exists with both policies attached
Instance profile exists and has the role

plan.text: Launch t2.micro instance (Amazon Linux 2023 AMI) with unpack-sg security group and unpack-ec2-role IAM profile. Allocate and associate Elastic IP. Verify SSH connectivity.

acceptance_criteria: Security group with SSH/HTTP/HTTPS inbound rules exists
EC2 key pair created and .pem stored securely
IAM role with CloudWatch + SSM permissions attached to instance
t2.micro instance running with Elastic IP

context.notes: Shaky skills: Deployment, Security — extra detail on every step. All commands should be AWS CLI. Use default VPC (every new AWS account has one). Amazon Linux 2023 uses dnf, not yum.
